title: Positioning: The Battle for Your Mind
subtitle: How to Be Seen and Heard in the Overcrowded Marketplace
themes: The battle is for the mind, not the product
The mental ladder: how the mind organizes categories
First in the mind wins — leader's advantage
If you cannot be first in a category, create a new one
Reposition competitors to make room for your idea
The name is the hook that carries the position
Line extension is the most common and most damaging mistake
Sacrifice and saying no are the essence of positioning
whoShouldRead: Founders and CEOs deciding what their company stands for
Marketers and brand managers shaping category perception
Product managers evaluating roadmaps through a positioning lens
Agency and creative teams crafting clear, single-idea messages
Entrepreneurs entering or creating a new category
Investors assessing whether a company owns a defensible mental position
whoShouldSkip: Readers wanting a digital or social media playbook (the book predates the web)
Those looking for quantitative market research methods (it is anecdote-driven)
Practitioners seeking a modern, agile positioning process for B2B SaaS
Anyone allergic to repetition and dogmatic tone
keyIdeas: Positioning is what you do to the mind of the prospect, not what you do to the product
The mind is a ladder: each category holds a ranked set of brands and the top rungs are nearly unassailable
It is better to be first than to be better — first entrants hold roughly 2:1 market share over the runner-up
If you cannot be first in an existing category, create a new category you can own (a creneau)
To put a new idea in the mind you must first move the old one out — reposition the competition
The name is the single most important marketing decision; weak names (initials, generics) lose the position
Line extension dilutes the parent brand — one name cannot stand for two different things
Sometimes the right positioning decision is to not enter the market at all — sacrifice is the essence of positioning
keyTakeaways: Marketing is a battle of perceptions, not products
Work with what is already in the mind; do not fight it
Being first in a category compounds; the leader's advantage is structural, not circumstantial
Find a hole the leader has ignored (size, price, gender, distribution, occasion) and own it
Repositioning works by making the old idea look outdated or wrong
A great name hints at the category or benefit and travels through every medium unchanged
A line extension is a teeter-totter: lift the new product and you drop the original
Saying no to attractive adjacencies protects the position you already own
excerpt: The battle for your market is fought in the prospect's mind, not on the shelf. Positioning shows that the only winning move is to own a simple, distinctive idea in the mind — and that being first in a category, or creating a new one, beats being merely better.
